{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"build_scheduler","new":"","old":"","op":"replace_function","source":"def build_scheduler(optimizer: optim.Optimizer, steps_per_epoch: int):
    if not USE_LR_SCHEDULE:
        return None
    total_steps = TRAIN_MAX_STEPS if TRAIN_MAX_STEPS is not None else steps_per_epoch * 100
    total_steps = max(1, total_steps)
    warmup_steps = min(max(1, total_steps // 10), 20)
    cosine_steps = max(1, total_steps - warmup_steps)
    warmup = optim.lr_scheduler.LinearLR(
        optimizer,
        start_factor=0.2,
        end_factor=1.0,
        total_iters=warmup_steps,
    )
    cosine = optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=cosine_steps)
    return optim.lr_scheduler.SequentialLR(
        optimizer,
        schedulers=[warmup, cosine],
        milestones=[warmup_steps],
    )","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"The current script disables scheduling and its cosine helper is misaligned with the fixed 256-step budget. A short warmup followed by cosine decay over the actual step budget is a simple way to improve optimization within the verifier limit.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The higher fixed learning rate helped, which suggests the current model is optimization-limited rather than unstable. A modest width increase in the convolution stack and classifier is a simple next step that should improve representation quality within the same shallow architecture without adding much complexity.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The modest width increase improved validation loss, so the model still appears capacity-limited. Adding one more convolutional downsampling block is the simplest next architecture change to increase feature hierarchy while keeping the rest of the script and optimizer stable.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 2","old":"DEPTH = 3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The extra depth regressed, so the stronger two-block model remains the better architecture. The next clean test is to keep that better layout and switch from Adam to AdamW, which often gives better short-budget generalization at the same learning rate and weight decay.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"AdamW underperformed the simpler Adam setup, so the strongest direction remains the two-block wider model with Adam. The next low-complexity test is a small learning-rate increase above the current best setting to see whether the short 256-step budget still benefits from more aggressive optimization.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last step suggests the stronger model prefers the original 1e-3 rate. With that restored, the next useful short-budget test is a larger batch so each of the fixed 256 optimizer steps sees more data, which can improve validation loss without changing the core architecture.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The jump from batch size 64 to 128 produced a large gain, which suggests the fixed-step budget benefits from processing more examples per optimizer step. The cleanest next experiment is to continue in that direction with batch size 256 while keeping the now-strong architecture and optimizer unchanged.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The main gain now comes from the much larger batch, which usually supports a somewhat larger step size. A moderate learning-rate increase is the simplest next test to better match optimization scale to batch size 256 without changing the architecture or adding new training logic.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Increasing batch size has been the strongest lever so far by letting the fixed 256-step budget cover more training examples. The next clean test is batch size 512, keeping the now-strong learning rate and architecture unchanged to see whether additional data per step continues to reduce validation loss.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch size has been the dominant source of improvement across several consecutive steps, which indicates this fixed-step verifier strongly rewards covering more examples per optimizer update. The next direct test is batch size 1024, keeping the rest of the optimized configuration unchanged.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Batch-size scaling has continued to help, and the current large-batch regime usually tolerates a somewhat larger learning rate. A modest increase to 2e-3 is the simplest next optimization change that could improve convergence without increasing model complexity or further extending the runtime per step.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"At batch size 1024 the model is effectively trained for several passes over the data, so mild extra regularization is a sensible next move. Restoring the better 1.5e-3 learning rate and increasing weight decay is a simple way to test whether generalization improves in this large-batch regime.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Extra regularization hurt, so the stronger large-batch setting still prefers the lighter weight decay. Given how much more data this regime processes, the next simple test is a modest width increase from the current best architecture to see whether additional capacity can convert that extra signal into lower validation loss.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimizer tweak did not help, so the best direction remains the original Adam settings with large-batch training. A larger hidden layer is a simpler capacity increase than widening the convolution stack and adds little compute, making it a reasonable late-stage layout test.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Increasing the classifier hidden size from 64 to 128 improved loss while keeping the convolutional backbone unchanged, suggesting the head still has useful capacity headroom. A further increase to 256 is a cheap follow-up layout test because it adds little runtime compared with widening the convolution stack.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The classifier head continues to respond well to added capacity, with larger hidden sizes improving validation loss without changing the convolutional backbone or optimizer. {"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 1024","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Classifier capacity has improved validation loss at every recent increase, including the latest move to 512.
